content:
First, observe that for any positive integer \(n\), the product of its digits is at most \(9^k\) if \(n\) has \(k\) digits, while 
\[
n^2-15n-27\sim n^2
\]
grows much faster.  In fact one checks:

1)  If \(n\) is 1‐digit, say \(n=d\in\{1,\dots,9\}\), then the digit‐product is \(d\), and the equation
\[
d \;=\; n^2-15n-27 \;=\; d^2-15d-27
\]
becomes 
\[
0=d^2-16d-27,
\]
which has no integer solution \(1\le d\le9\).

2)  If \(n\) is 2‐digit, write \(n=10a+b\) with \(1\le a\le9,\;0\le b\le9\).  Then the digit‐product is \(ab\), and
\[
ab \;=\;(10a+b)^2-15(10a+b)-27
\]
simplifies to the quadratic in \(b\),
\[
b^2+(19a-15)b+(100a^2-150a-27)=0.
\]
Its discriminant is 
\[
D=(19a-15)^2-4(100a^2-150a-27)
=-39a^2+30a+333.
\]
Checking \(a=1,2,3,\dots\) shows that the only time \(D\) is a perfect square \((18^2)\) is \(a=1\), and then
\[
b=\frac{-(19\cdot1-15)\pm18}{2}=\frac{-4\pm18}{2}
\]
gives \(b=7\) (the other root is negative).  Hence the only 2‐digit solution is
\[
n=17,
\]
for which \(1\cdot7=7\) and indeed \(17^2-15\cdot17-27=289-255-27=7.\)

3)  For \(n\) of 3 or more digits, one checks \(n^2-15n-27\) already exceeds the maximum possible digit‐product.  Thus there are no further solutions.

Conclusion: the only positive integer satisfying the condition is \(n=17\), and hence the requested sum is
\[
\boxed{17}.
\]
